annotations.label: Golden ratio (lead definition)

anchor.snippet: two quantities are in the golden ratio if their ratio is the same as the ratio of their sum to the larger of the two quantities

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the numeric constant φ = (1+√5)/2 but does not formalize the abstract relational definition on two quantities.

annotations.label: Golden rectangle cut into square and similar rectangle

anchor.snippet: may be cut into a square and a smaller rectangle with the same aspect ratio

annotations.note: No formalization of golden rectangles or the square-removal self-similarity property is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Golden ratio of two quantities

anchor.snippet: Two non-zero quantities

annotations.note: Only the specific real constant φ is defined; the general defining proportion (a+b)/a = a/b is not stated as a predicate.

annotations.label: Positive root is the golden ratio

anchor.snippet: The positive root

annotations.note: Mathlib proves φ² = φ + 1 together with `goldenRatio_pos`, witnessing φ as the positive root of x² − x − 1.

annotations.label: Euclid's extreme and mean ratio

anchor.snippet: A straight line is said to have been cut in extreme and mean ratio when, as the whole line is to the greater segment, so is the greater to the lesser.

annotations.note: Euclid's geometric segment definition (line cut in extreme and mean ratio) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Irrationality of the golden ratio

anchor.snippet: The golden ratio is an irrational number

annotations.note: Mathlib's `goldenRatio_irrational` directly states `Irrational φ`.

annotations.label: Irrationality via lowest-terms contradiction

anchor.section: Contradiction from an expression in lowest terms
anchor.snippet: This is a proof by infinite descent

annotations.note: Mathlib proves irrationality via √5 instead of the lowest-terms infinite-descent argument.

annotations.label: Irrationality via sqrt(5)

anchor.section: By irrationality of the square root of 5
anchor.snippet: Another short proof

annotations.note: The Mathlib proof of `goldenRatio_irrational` is precisely the argument deducing irrationality from `Nat.Prime.irrational_sqrt 5`.

annotations.label: Minimal polynomial of the golden ratio

anchor.snippet: the polynomial of lowest degree with integer coefficients that has the golden ratio as a root

annotations.note: Mathlib shows φ satisfies X² − X − 1 = 0 but has no explicit `minpoly ℤ φ = X² - X - 1` statement.

annotations.label: Both roots are algebraic integers

anchor.snippet: both roots are algebraic integers

annotations.note: No `IsIntegral ℤ φ`/`IsIntegral ℤ ψ` statement asserting that the golden ratio and its conjugate are algebraic integers is present.

annotations.label: Golden ratio as fundamental unit of golden field

anchor.snippet: is a fundamental unit of the quadratic field

annotations.note: The ring of integers ℤ[φ] and its fundamental unit structure for ℚ(√5) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Golden ratio is constructible

anchor.snippet: the golden ratio is a constructible number

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theory of straightedge-and-compass constructible numbers identifying φ as constructible.

annotations.label: Unique property among positive numbers

anchor.snippet: This illustrates the unique property of the golden ratio among positive numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib proves φ⁻¹ = −ψ = φ − 1, but does not state a uniqueness theorem characterizing φ as the unique positive number with x⁻¹ = x − 1.

annotations.label: Powers satisfy Fibonacci-like recurrence

anchor.snippet: is equal to the sum of the two immediately preceding powers

annotations.note: `goldenRatio_pow_sub_goldenRatio_pow` states φ^(n+2) − φ^(n+1) = φ^n, the Fibonacci-like recurrence on powers.

annotations.label: Simple continued fraction expansion

anchor.snippet: can be expanded recursively to obtain a simple continued fraction for the golden ratio

annotations.note: The continued fraction expansion [1;1,1,1,…] of the golden ratio is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hurwitz inequality for Diophantine approximations

anchor.snippet: Hurwitz inequality for Diophantine approximations

annotations.note: Hurwitz's theorem on Diophantine approximation (constant 1/√5) is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continued square root form

anchor.snippet: A continued square root form

annotations.note: The nested radical expression φ = √(1 + √(1 + ...)) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Fibonacci sequence recurrence

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: In the Fibonacci sequence, each term

annotations.note: `Nat.fib_add_two` proves `fib (n+2) = fib n + fib (n+1)` for Mathlib's `Nat.fib`.

annotations.label: Lucas numbers

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: The sequence of Lucas numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Lucas number sequence; `Nat.Choose.Lucas` and `LucasPrimality` are unrelated namesakes.

annotations.label: Golden ratio as limit of Fibonacci/Lucas ratios

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: the golden ratio is equal to the limit of the ratios of successive terms in the Fibonacci sequence and sequence of Lucas numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Fibonacci ratio limit equals φ, but the Lucas-number version is absent.

annotations.label: Closed-form (Binet's) expressions

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: Closed-form expressions for the Fibonacci and Lucas sequences that involve the golden ratio

annotations.note: `Real.coe_fib_eq` gives Binet's formula for Fibonacci numbers; the Lucas closed form Lₙ = φⁿ + ψⁿ is not formalized.

annotations.label: Limit of Lucas/Fibonacci quotient equals sqrt(5)

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: the limit of the quotient of Lucas numbers by Fibonacci numbers as equal to the square root of five

annotations.note: Without Lucas numbers in Mathlib, this limit statement cannot be formalized.

annotations.label: Powers obey Fibonacci recurrence

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: Successive powers of the golden ratio obey the Fibonacci recurrence

annotations.note: `geom_goldenRatio_isSol_fibRec` states the geometric sequence n ↦ φⁿ is a solution of the Fibonacci linear recurrence.

annotations.label: Sum of third consecutive Fibonacci is Lucas

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: the sum of third consecutive Fibonacci numbers equals a Lucas number

annotations.note: Identity Fₙ₋₁ + Fₙ₊₁ = Lₙ is not formalized; Lucas numbers are absent.

annotations.label: Construction by interior division

anchor.snippet: Dividing by interior division

annotations.note: Compass-and-straightedge construction by interior division is not formalized.

annotations.label: Construction by exterior division

anchor.snippet: Dividing by exterior division

annotations.note: Compass-and-straightedge construction by exterior division is not formalized.

annotations.label: Golden angle

anchor.snippet: the smaller is called the golden angle

annotations.note: The golden angle 2π/φ² is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diagonal-to-side ratio of regular pentagon

anchor.snippet: In a regular pentagon the ratio of a diagonal to a side is the golden ratio

annotations.note: Regular pentagons and their diagonal-to-side ratio are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Golden triangle (sublime triangle)

anchor.snippet: is called a golden triangle or sublime triangle

annotations.note: No definition of golden / sublime triangle exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Golden gnomon

anchor.snippet: is called a golden gnomon

annotations.note: No definition of the golden gnomon triangle exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bisection subdivides golden triangle

anchor.snippet: Bisecting one of the base angles of the golden triangle subdivides it into a smaller golden triangle and a golden gnomon

annotations.note: This subdivision result has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trisection subdivides golden gnomon

anchor.snippet: If the apex angle of the golden gnomon is trisected

annotations.note: This subdivision result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Odom's construction from equilateral triangle

anchor.snippet: George Odom found a construction

annotations.note: Odom's construction of φ from an equilateral triangle is not formalized.

annotations.label: Kepler triangle

anchor.snippet: is the unique right triangle with sides in geometric progression

annotations.note: The Kepler triangle (sides 1 : √φ : φ) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximum inradius-to-side ratio isosceles triangle

anchor.snippet: Among isosceles triangles, the ratio of inradius to side length is maximized for the triangle formed by two reflected copies of the Kepler triangle

annotations.note: Extremal isosceles inradius-to-side ratio result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Golden rectangle

anchor.snippet: The golden ratio proportions the adjacent side lengths of a golden rectangle

annotations.note: Golden rectangles are not defined as a notion in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Golden rhombus

anchor.snippet: A golden rhombus is a rhombus whose diagonals are in proportion to the golden ratio

annotations.note: Golden rhombi are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vesica piscis golden subdivision

anchor.snippet: is subdivided in the golden ratio

annotations.note: Vesica piscis and the related φ subdivision are not formalized.

annotations.label: Golden spiral

anchor.snippet: A logarithmic spiral whose radius increases by a factor of the golden ratio for each quarter-turn is called the golden spiral

annotations.note: Golden / logarithmic spirals are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three golden rectangles form Borromean rings

anchor.snippet: Sets of three golden rectangles intersect perpendicularly inside dodecahedra and icosahedra, forming Borromean rings

annotations.note: Dodecahedra, icosahedra, and Borromean ring configurations are not formalized.

annotations.label: Cube inscribed in dodecahedron

anchor.snippet: A cube can be inscribed in a regular dodecahedron

annotations.note: Inscribed-cube-in-dodecahedron geometry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Newton's method computation time

anchor.snippet: The time needed to compute

annotations.note: Computational complexity bounds for evaluating φ by Newton's method are not formalized.

annotations.label: Fifth roots of unity sum is quadratic integer

anchor.snippet: the sum of any fifth root of unity and its complex conjugate

annotations.note: Identity 2cos(2π/5) = (√5 − 1)/2 connecting fifth roots of unity to φ is not formalized.

annotations.label: Gamma function equation solutions

anchor.snippet: the only solutions to the equation

annotations.note: The functional-equation characterization of φ via the Gamma function is not formalized.

annotations.label: Terminating representations in golden base

anchor.snippet: have terminating representations, but rational fractions have non-terminating representations

annotations.note: Base-φ (phinary) numeral representations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Golden ratio in hyperbolic geometry

anchor.snippet: The golden ratio also appears in hyperbolic geometry

annotations.note: Hyperbolic-geometry appearances of φ are not formalized.

annotations.label: Golden ratio in modular functions

anchor.snippet: The golden ratio appears in the theory of modular functions

annotations.note: Modular-function identities involving φ (e.g. via Rogers–Ramanujan) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Golden ratio is a Pisot–Vijayaraghavan number

anchor.snippet: is a Pisot–Vijayaraghavan number

annotations.note: Pisot–Vijayaraghavan numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadratic equation satisfied by φ

anchor.snippet: satisfies the quadratic equation

annotations.note: `goldenRatio_sq` proves φ² = φ + 1, equivalently φ² − φ − 1 = 0.

annotations.label: Powers of φ round to Lucas numbers

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: the powers of

annotations.note: The statement that φⁿ rounds to Lₙ (for n ≥ 2) is not formalized; Lucas numbers are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polynomial in φ reduces to linear expression

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: This identity allows any polynomial in

annotations.note: The general reduction of polynomials in φ to a linear expression aφ + b (with Fibonacci coefficients) is not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Convergents alternate above and below φ

anchor.section: Relationship to Fibonacci and Lucas numbers
anchor.snippet: These approximations are alternately lower and higher

annotations.note: The alternating-above-and-below behavior of Fibonacci/Lucas successive-ratio convergents to φ is not formalized.
